coredata.dc:title: Green nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots derived from eggshell as a high performance aptasensing interface for non-invasive detection of metronidazole
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Ghiasi, Maryam
Ghanbarzadeh, Mahsa
Ghaffarinejad, Ali
Shahdost-Fard, Faezeh
coredata.dc:description:
Background The concentration monitoring of the banned metronidazole (MTZ) drug as the alarmed carcinogenic agent in human biofluids is medically essential. The electrochemical aptasensors are good candidates to overcome some presence challenges in the detection process.
Results Herein, an electrochemical aptasensor based on nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (NcS-CDs) has been developed for the high-sensitive detection of MTZ for the first time. The N–S-CDs have been synthesized from eggshell waste and the aptasensing interface has been fabricated by the attachment of the N–S-CDs to the glassy carbon electrode surface to fix the high-density aptamer (Apt) sequences via silver nanocubes (AgNCs). The electrochemical signal of the redox probe has changed by trapping the MTZ on the Apt's arm onto the aptasensing interface. The molecular dynamic simulation visually confirmed how the Apt binds to the MTZ. The resulting aptasensor measured MTZ in a wide linear range value from 1 fM to 901.4 nM with a limit of detection value of 333.3 aM and high selectivity regarding some possible interferences.
Significance The results reveal that the novel aptasensor has high potential applicability for the non-invasive monitoring of MTZ in human saliva and urine, and also, the tap water analysis. Utilizing waste-derived nanomaterials for the aptasensor fabrication not only less the fabrication costs but also guarantees the greenness of the applied methodology according to sustainable chemistry principles as evaluated by two international index metrics.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Metronidazole
Waste-derived nanomaterial
Eggshell
Carbon dots
Silver nanocubes
Electrochemical aptasensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The preparation of the real samples
Structural and morphological characterization of the synthesized N–S-CDs
Morphological and structural characterization of the synthesized AgNCs
Morphological and structural characterization of the AgNCs/N–S-CDs nanocomposite attached to the GCE surface
Electrochemical characterization of the Apt/AgNCs/N–S-CDs/GCE surface as the sensing interface
The optimum condition for the experiment
Analytical performance evaluation of the MTZ aptasensor
Evaluation of the stability, selectivity, repeatability, and reproducibility of the aptasensor
Greenness profile of the proposed strategy

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to thank and acknowledge the great support from Iran University of Science and Technology (IUST) and Farhangian University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) FT-IR spectra of the ESM and synthesized N–S-CDs, (B) UV–Vis and (C) fluorescence spectra with corresponding colors, (D and E) HR-TEM images with different magnifications and (F) FFT pattern of the synthesized N–S-CDs, (G) FT-IR spectra of AgNO3 and synthesized AgNCs, (H) HR-TEM image with size distribution graph and (I) XRD pattern of the synthesized AgNCs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
FE-SEM images of the (A) unmodified GCE, (B) N–S-CDs/GCE, and (C) AgNCs/N–S-CDs/GCE. (D) EDX spectrum and (E) elemental mapping analysis of the AgNCs/N–S-CDs/GCE surface.
MD simulated interaction of MTZ with Apt sequence using Gromacs software during 100 ns.

(A) CVs (at a scan rate of 100 mV s−1), (B) DPVs and (C) Nyquist curves (at 230 mV with an equivalent circuit) of the different electrodes at each stage of the electrode modification process (a: GCE, b: N–S-CDs/GCE, c: AgNCs/N–S-CDs/GCE, d: Apt/AgNCs/N–S-CDs/GCE and e: 1 pM MTZ/BSA/Apt/AgNCs/N–S-CDs/GCE). Effect of (D) different amounts and (E) various times for the N–S-CDs attachment onto the GCE surface on the recorded signal. (F) Effect of the incubation time of MTZ (1 pM) on the aptasensor surface. (G) The DPVs of the aptasensor after incubating a series of different concentrations of MTZ including 1 fM, 300 fM, 600 fM, 900 fM, 1.2 pM, 301.2 pM, 601.2 pM, 901.2 pM, 1.4 nM, 301.4 nM, 601.4 nM and 901.4 nM (from top to bottom) and (H) the calibration curves of the aptasensor signal versus different MTZ concentrations (nM). (I) Bar plots correspond to the recorded response after applying the 1st and 80th consecutive repetitive cycles. (Electrolyte: 0.1 M of PB solution (pH = 7.4) containing 5 mM of [Fe(CN6)]3-/4- (at the same ratio) and 0.1 M of KCl as the anion redox probe).

A cartoon presentation of the process of (A) the synthesis of N–S-CDs, (B) AgNCs and (C) the aptasensor fabrication based on the AgNCs/N–S-CDs nanocomposite for MTZ detection. Evaluation of the environmental friendliness of the proposed method using the international (D) ComplexGAPI and (E) AGREE metrics.

head.ce:title: Green nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots derived from eggshell as a high performance aptasensing interface for non-invasive detection of metronidazole

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Laboratory of Real Samples Analysis, Faculty of Chemistry, Iran University of Science and Technology (IUST), Tehran, 1684613114, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Laboratory of Real Samples Analysis
Iran University of Science and Technology (IUST)

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Electroanalytical Chemistry Research Center, Iran University of Science and Technology (IUST), Tehran, 1684613114, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Electroanalytical Chemistry Research Center, Iran University of Science and Technology (IUST), Tehran, 1684613114, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry Education, Farhangian University, P.O. Box 14665-889, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry Education, Farhangian University, P.O. Box 14665-889, Tehran, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Research Laboratory of Real Samples Analysis, Faculty of Chemistry, Iran University of Science and Technology (IUST), Tehran, 1684613114, Iran.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Laboratory of Real Samples Analysis
Iran University of Science and Technology (IUST)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The concentration monitoring of the banned metronidazole (MTZ) drug as the alarmed carcinogenic agent in human biofluids is medically essential. The electrochemical aptasensors are good candidates to overcome some presence challenges in the detection process.

Herein, an electrochemical aptasensor based on nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (NcS-CDs) has been developed for the high-sensitive detection of MTZ for the first time. The N–S-CDs have been synthesized from eggshell waste and the aptasensing interface has been fabricated by the attachment of the N–S-CDs to the glassy carbon electrode surface to fix the high-density aptamer (Apt) sequences via silver nanocubes (AgNCs). The electrochemical signal of the redox probe has changed by trapping the MTZ on the Apt's arm onto the aptasensing interface. The molecular dynamic simulation visually confirmed how the Apt binds to the MTZ. The resulting aptasensor measured MTZ in a wide linear range value from 1 fM to 901.4 nM with a limit of detection value of 333.3 aM and high selectivity regarding some possible interferences.

The results reveal that the novel aptasensor has high potential applicability for the non-invasive monitoring of MTZ in human saliva and urine, and also, the tap water analysis. Utilizing waste-derived nanomaterials for the aptasensor fabrication not only less the fabrication costs but also guarantees the greenness of the applied methodology according to sustainable chemistry principles as evaluated by two international index metrics.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Despite the advantages of metronidazole (MTZ) in the treatment of diseases caused by anaerobic bacteria and protozoa its overdose (more than 140 mM) leads to serious side effects such as Stevens-Johnson syndrome, peripheral nerve damage, encephalopathy, neutropenia, and seizures. Additionally, using MTZ in the feed of some animals and the subsequent pollution in agricultural products and aquatic environments creates drug-resistant microorganisms [1]. Since this antibiotic has been alarmed as a carcinogenic agent based on the National Institute of Health reports and its use has been banned by the US Food and Drug Administration organization and the European Union [2], the accurate measurement of MTZ in human biofluids is medicolegally important. Nevertheless, despite some advantages, most of the reported MTZ sensors based on spectrophotometry, chromatography, and electrochemical methods are expensive, complicated, less selective, and time-consuming as listed in Table S-1. To overcome these challenges, combining the aptamer (Apt) as a highly selective bioreceptor towards its target and electrochemical strategies for fabricating the electrochemical aptasensors are actively studied owing to the advantages of making highly accurate and sensitive responses by a simple operation [3]. However, the electrochemical aptasensor for MTZ assay is rarely reported. It is worth noting that choosing an appropriate platform for the durable fixation of the Apt sequence in the amplified detection of biorecognition events is a major bottleneck in the aptasensor fabrication process. Various nanocomposites consisting of MXene [4,5], reduced graphene oxide [6], dendrimer [7], and metal-organic frameworks [8–10] have been recently reported in the design of different electrochemical aptasensors.

In recent years, considerable research efforts have been effectively made on waste recycling to introduce the new generation of alternative green and low-cost nanomaterials as value-added products with less potential toxicity and much bioavailability properties [11]. Eggshell (ES) ranked as the 15th major food industry pollution problem by the Environmental Protection Agency, has been reported to be the most abundant agricultural (hatchery), industrial, and domestic natural waste with approximately 8.58 million tons in 2018 [12]. Discarded ES destructively leads to different types of soil, water, and air pollution and dangerously threatens public health because of fungal growth on their surface and attracting vermin on the protein-rich membranous part [13]. Apart from its use as the fertilizer or ingredient for animal feed, this inorganic and organic-rich waste has been beneficially applied in various industries such as cosmetics, tissue engineering, and absorbent for hazardous chemicals [14]. In the hunt for waste materials as green resources in generating non-toxic, low-cost, and value-added nanostructures, ES membrane (ESM) has been utilized for synthesizing carbon dots (CDs) [15]. CDs, nanomaterials with sizes of less than 10 nm, have presented fantastic electrochemical/optical advantages in various applications [16]. The synthesis of CDs based on green precursor has boosted their efficiency regarding other precursors like thiourea, carbon nanotubes, and ethylene glycol (EG) by some side effects [17]. Several crucial factors such as the temperature and time of reaction, and the type of precursor materials, as well as the carbon amount, significantly affect the heteroatom co-doping process into CDs and its surface passivation [18]. Since ESM is mainly composed of fibrous proteins, glycosaminoglycans, and sulfated glycoproteins [19] the ESM-synthesis strategy co-inserts nitrogen (N) and sulfur (S) heteroatoms on the CDs’ surface compared other green precursors (fruits, waste papers, and plant leaves) [20,21]. Besides its optical advantage improvement, the heteroatom inserting process empowers the electrochemical properties of the resulting nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N–S-CDs) to fix the Apt sequence on its surface via a linker through chemical binding. Unitizing the N–S-CDs as a novel efficient sub-layer in this study is helpful for high-dense loading of Apt onto the electrode surface in the electrochemical aptasensor fabrication.

Various nanomaterials-based cross-linkers (nanogolds [22], nanosilvers [23] and nanonickels [24]) have mainly offered active surface area enhancement and electron transfer amplification for the targeted binding facilitation of Apt sequence on the surface compared to covalent cross-linkers (glutaraldehyde [25] and 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethyl aminopropyl) carbodiimide/N-hydroxysuccinimide) [26]. Among diverse shapes of nanosilvers with chemical, photochemical, and microwave-based synthesis routes, silver nanocubes (AgNCs) as a cost-effective and beneficial class of nanostructures ensures Apt attaching on the embedded surface through its facets by Ag–S and Ag–N bonds [27].

Our motivation in this study is a novel integration of the ESM-derived N–S-CDs with AgNCs as the new efficient AgNCs/N–S-CDs nanocomposite for the Apt attaching on the electrode surface to fabricate the electrochemical MTZ aptasensor. By emphasizing the reducing costs and smart use of waste-based nanomaterials according to the evaluated green chemistry international criteria, the present study is the first report on the electrochemical aptasensor fabrication for highly-sensitive measurement of MTZ in human biofluids and tap water according to the author's knowledge.

The amino Apt sequence highly specific to MTZ containing 15 bases (5′–NH2–(CH2)6CTGTTTGGTAGGCAG-3′) [28] was bought from Nedayefan Company (Iran). Chicken eggs were purchased from a local market in Tehran city (Iran). MTZ, amoxicillin (C16H19N3O5S, AMX), cephalexin (C16H17N3O4S, CFX), folic acid (C19H19N7O6, FA), and penicillin G (C16H18N2O4S, Pen G) were obtained from Raha and Farabi Companies (Iran). The drugs were dissolved in DI water and different concentrations of them were diluted from a stock solution. Silver nitrate (AgNO3), sodium sulfide (Na2S.9H₂O), hydrochloric acid (HCl), polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP), EG, glacial acid acetic (CH3COOH), bovine serum albumin (BSA), uric acid (C5H4N4O3, UA) and all other chemical reagents without further purification were ordered from Sigma-Aldrich and Merck Companies (Germany). 0.1 M phosphate buffer (PB) solution (pH = 7.4) containing 5.0 mM potassium ferrocyanide (K4Fe(CN)6) and potassium ferricyanide (K3Fe(CN)6) at the same ratio and 0.1 M potassium chloride (KCl) was used as the redox electrochemical probe with the name of ferro/ferricyanide in all of the electrochemical experiments. All experiments were done at 25 °C ± 0.1.

The molecular dynamics (MD) computational method was performed using Gromacs 2022.6 software to predict the binding behavior of Apt and MTZ. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) studies were investigated by a type Ⅱ (μ-AUTOLAB) Potentiostat/Galvanostat instrument (Eco-Chemie, Switzerland). The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) analysis was done with PGSTAT 204 N Potentiostat/Galvanostat instrument equipped with NOVA 2.1 software. The electrochemical setup was based on a three-electrode system including a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) as the working electrode, silver/silver chloride (Ag/AgCl, 3 M KCl) as the reference electrode, and platinum (Pt) wire as the counter electrode. All images related to the field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), energy dispersive X-ray spectrometry (EDS), and elemental mapping were recorded by a TESCAN instrument, Mira III LMU (Czech Republic). The functional groups of the compounds were investigated by recording Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum via a PerkinElmer spectrophotometer. A Bruker AXS (D8 model) instrument with Cu Kα radiation by a generator operating at 40 kV and 150 mA was used for recording X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum was obtained by a Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrometer. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) and Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) images were recorded by a FEI TEC9G20 instrument at 200 kV and a PerkinElmer spectrophotometer (Spectrum 100 model, USA), respectively. Fluorescence analysis was recorded by PL spectroscopy (G9800A, Agilent).

The N–S-CDs were synthesized utilizing ESM under a simple route based on the literature [8] with some modifications (Scheme. 1 A). The contents of five white chicken eggs were taken out and all pieces of the ESs were washed carefully with DI water. The obtained clean ESs were treated with CH3COOH (50 % v/v in DI water) for one day to dissolve the calcium carbonate layer. The extracted ESMs were carefully collected and washed with DI water several times and then, dried at room temperature. 200.00 mg of the dried ESM was heated in a muffle furnace at 300 °C (4 h) and then, the resulting black ESM ash was ground in a mortar to obtain a fine black powder. Next, 110.00 mg of the fine powder was dispersed in 250.00 mL DI water and then, sonicated in an ultra-sonication bath for 2 h. With centrifuging the obtained mixture at 5000 rpm and removing some large particles, the N–S-CDs were finally obtained and then, stored at 4 °C [21].

AgNCs were easily synthesized by a quick sulfide-mediated polyol method (Scheme. 1B). Briefly, 30.00 mL of EG was heated at 152 °C under stirring for 1 h and 420 μL of Na2S.9H2O (3 mM in EG) was quickly added to it. After 8 min, 7.50 mL of PVP (20.00 mg mL−1 in EG) and 2.50 mL of AgNO3 (48.00 mg mL−1 in EG) were separately added to the mixture to make a solution with a yellowish-brown color. The resulting uniform AgNCs were cooled and then, stored in a dark place at room temperature [15,29].

The aptasensor was fabricated based on the GCE surface as the initial platform (Scheme. 1C). Firstly, the GCE surface was carefully polished on a pad impregnated with alumina slurry to obtain a mirror surface. The cleaned GCE surface without residual particles was obtained by sonication in a mixture of DI water and ethanol with the same ratio (5 min) and several washing steps. Next, 10 μL of the synthesized N–S-CDs was dropped onto the clean GCE surface (3 h) to obtain the N–S-CDs/GCE. In the next step, 10 μL of AgNCs was immobilized on the N–S-CDs/GCE surface (2 h) via bonding of N and S linkers of the N–S-CDs. Then, 10 μL of the Apt sequence (5 μM) was fixed on the embedded AgNCs/N–S-CDs/GCE surface for 6 h to obtain the Apt/AgNCs/N–S-CDs/GCE. The possible remaining sites of AgNCs/N–S-CDs/GCE surface without Apt sequence were blocked by adding 10 μL of BSA (1 %, w/v) on the surface (20 min). Finally, the fabricated BSA/Apt/AgNCs/N–S-CDs/GCE denoted as the aptasensor, was stored at 4 °C to use for the next experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The preparation of the real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Some human biofluids including urine and saliva were randomly collected from a healthy 25-year-old girl with fasting condition according to ethical principles. First, 1 mL of urine was diluted with 4 mL of 0.1 M PB (pH = 7.4) and then, three different concentrations of MTZ were added to the diluted urine samples. The saliva sample was also prepared with a similar method at a dilution ratio of 1–2 (sample: PB). The tap water sample was collected from home tap water. Before analysis, 30 mL of the filtered sample was combined with various amounts of the standard solution of MTZ, and the mixture was diluted with DI water to 50 mL. Finally, 10 μL of each prepared sample with three concentrations of MTZ (1 fM, 1.2 pM, and 1.4 nM) for each matrix was independently drop-casted onto the prepared aptasensor surface under the optimum condition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and morphological characterization of the synthesized N–S-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: N–S-CDs, as a pivotal component in the aptasensor fabrication, were synthesized from ESM via calcination and sonication-assisted green synthesis. FT-IR spectra of the ESM as the initial material and N–S-CDs as the product were recorded to characterize the synthesized N–S-CDs. As exhibited in Fig. 1 A, the ESM spectrum shows some short peaks around 1401 cm−1 that are attributed to the presence of carbonate groups in the ESM matrix [30,31]. The detectable peak at 621 cm−1 may be assigned to the in-plane and out-of-plane deformation modes related to the calcium carbonate [32]. Other peaks located at 3288 cm−1 and 1621 cm−1 may be attributed to the amine and amide groups in the ESM. In contrast, the FT-IR spectrum of the N–S-CDs exhibits some peaks in the range of 750 cm−1 and 1150 cm−1 that may be assigned to the bonds of the C–S and C–S–C, respectively. The peak located at 1440 cm−1 can be attributed to the CC stretching mode. Two peaks located at 1715 cm−1 and 1640 cm−1 are corroborated by the stretching mode of CO related to the acidic and amide linkages in the N–S-CDs. The broad peaks at 3400 cm−1 and 3170 cm−1 may correspond to the –OH and –NH groups in the N–S-CDs [15]. A comparison between the location and intensity of the peaks in both spectra of ESM and the synthesized N–S-CDs reveals the successful synthesis of N–S-CDs with the co-doped heteroatoms is helpful in the attachment of the further layer in the aptasensor fabrication. The UV–Vis spectrum of N–S-CDs in Fig. 1B displays two absorbance bands around 210 nm and 310 nm under visible light that may be assigned to π–π∗ (sp2 hybridized carbon) and n–π∗ transitions of the carbon nanostructures, respectively [33]. The transparent solution of N–S-CDs with a dark yellow color (inset of Fig. 1B) was stable for more than five months without any aggregation. The fluorescence spectrum of the N–S-CDs dispersed in DI water in Fig. 1C indicates an emission peak at 505 nm upon exposure to an excitation wavelength of 410 nm. The blue color of the N–S-CDs under UV light (inset of Fig. 1C) may be attributed to the n–π∗ transitions based on the literature [28]. Furthermore, HR-TEM images with two magnifications in Fig. 1D and E manifest the nanosheets shape of the synthesized N–S-CDs. Additionally, the FFT pattern related to a single N–S-CDs in Fig. 1F shows a discriminated crystalline structure with the existence of (002) and (101) lattice planes of graphitic carbon [15].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological and structural characterization of the synthesized AgNCs
ce:section.ce:para: AgNO3 was converted to Ag in the green and quick synthesis process of AgNCs by EG and PVP. Fig. 1G displays the FT-IR spectra of AgNO3 as the primary substance and AgNCs as the synthesized nanostructures. The peak at 3430 cm−1 is indicative of significant O–H stretching vibrations in both AgNO3 and AgNCs. A wide peak at 1385 cm−1 may be related to the NO bond in the NO3-stretching vibration of AgNO3. In contrast, the AgNCs indicate bonds at 2865 cm−1 and 2950 cm−1 may be assigned to the carboxylic/phenolic groups. A sharp peak located at 3430 cm−1 may be related to strong and extensive O–H stretching of the carboxylic bonds. The existence of two amine vibrations is confirmed by 1645 cm−1 and 1473 cm−1. HR-TEM image and size variation graph of the resulting AgNCs in Fig. 1H validate the successful synthesis of monodispersed AgNCs with an average nanosize of less than 5 nm [34,35]. XRD pattern of AgNCs in Fig. 1I reveals four distinct diffraction peaks located at 2θ of 37.90°, 44.10°, 64.20°, and 77.20° attributing to the (111), (200), (220), and (311) planes of the Ag according to standard JCPDS, No. 04–0783 [36]. Notably, the peaks located at 37.90° and 44.10° correspond to the lattice planes (111) and (200) in the fcc arrangement of the Ag. Since (111) or (100) facets preferentially contain AgNCs forming certain free PVP molecules in the solution equilibrium the surface free energy of (100) and (111) facets is because of the specific binding of the PVP to them [37]. As a result, the surface exhibits crisp (111) facets and consequently, AgNCs are synthesized with a little corner truncation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological and structural characterization of the AgNCs/N–S-CDs nanocomposite attached to the GCE surface
ce:section.ce:para: A correct attachment of AgNCs/N–S-CDs nanocomposite on the GCE surface is a crucial step in the aptasensor fabrication process. The structural morphology characterization of the embedded sensing interface was monitored by FE-SEM analysis for different steps. The unmodified GCE surface exhibits a smooth and uniform surface with a small unevenness in Fig. 2 A while, the presence of the decentralized masses on the electrode surface in Fig. 2B confirms the N–S-CDs attached to the GCE surface. The surface morphology change in the presence of AgNCs in Fig. 2C confirms the successful immobilization of the AgNCs on the N–S-CDs layer via chemisorption linking between the co-doped heteroatoms with the AgNCs. The investigation of the EDX spectrum and the elemental mapping analysis of the AgNCs/N–S-CDs/GCE surface in Fig. 2D and E certifies the presence of C, O, Ag, Ca, S, and N elements unevenly distributed on the modified electrode surface. These findings corroborate that AgNCs/N–S-CDs nanocomposite has well adhered to the GCE surface.

The MD computational method provides beneficial information about biological compounds from the molecular viewpoint and complements the experimental methods by preventing incorrect interpretations of their results [38]. To visually predict the binding behavior of Apt and MTZ, MD simulation using Gromacs 2022.6 software was performed and simulations were carried out using a cut-off distance of 1.4 nm with a time step of 2 fs in a neutral environment. Periodic boundary conditions were introduced in all directions to implement the MD simulations. Finally, a 100 ns production MD simulation was performed for each system with a time step of 2 fs. As illustrated in Fig. 3 , the MD simulation results manifest the MTZ interacts with the middle of the Apt sequence, certifying the proper immobilizing of the 5′–NH2–terminal of the Apt onto the N–S-CDs/GCE in the aptasensor fabrication process. The MD simulation during 100 ns reveals that MTZ forms several bonds with the Apt sequence's adenine (A10) and guanine (G11) through various interactions consisting of three hydrogen bonds (H-bonds), two π-π stacking interactions, and one π-alkyl interaction.

Furthermore, the structural stability of the studied systems was investigated by analyzing the root mean square deviation (RMSD). Comparing the RMSD plot of the Apt interacted by MTZ in Fig. 1S–A and the RMSD plot related to alone MTZ in Fig. 1S–B confirms the interaction between the Apt and MTZ and the structural stability of them by the simulated systems after the MD simulation. Moreover, 100 ns was considered the proper time for obtaining precise results for further analyses. The Apt/target recognition relies on the balance between the Apt and the induced fit by the bind forming [39]. The residual flexibility of the backbone atoms of Apt and MTZ for the simulation (i.e., for their time-averaged positions) was investigated by Root Mean Square fluctuation (RMSF) plot. Inspecting the RMSF values of the Apt in Fig. 1S–C indicates that different regions of this sequence are endowed with different flexibility and less residual fluctuation in docked complexes manifested in the binding of the MTZ in the middle of the Apt sequence. All these results corroborate that the Apt sequence wraps around MTZ to link to it through the nucleotides of the middle side of the sequence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical characterization of the Apt/AgNCs/N–S-CDs/GCE surface as the sensing interface
ce:section.ce:para:
Designing a durable and stable sensing interface as a critical step in aptasensor fabrication guarantees the efficiency of the aptasensing approach. To examine the correct immobilization of each layer on the GCE surface the electrochemical behavior of the modified electrodes was monitored by CV technique in the [Fe(CN6)]3-/4- as the redox probe at a scan rate of 100 mV s−1. Fig. 4 A depicts a typical reversible voltammogram for the unmodified GCE surface (curve a) with an anodic current (I
p,a ) of 54 μA and cathodic current (I
p,c ) of −53 μA by a value of the peak-to-peak separation potential (ΔE) of 100 mV. In contrast, the voltammogram of the N–S-CDs/GCE (curve b) shows less I
p,a (47 μA) and I
p,c (−45 μA) values by a more ΔE value of 140 mV. This decrease in the electron transfer ability may be attributed to the N–S-CDs layer with the co-doped heteroatoms by the partial negative charge that repels the redox probe on the surface. Voltammogram of the AgNCs/N–S-CDs/GCE surface (curve c) exhibits a decrease in the I
p,a (39 μA) and I
p,c (−38 μA) values with an increase in ΔE value (210 mV) compared to the previous layers. This electrochemical behavior confirms the AgNCs immobilization on the N–S-CDs through a self-assembled monolayer (SAM) attachment via the chemisorption linkage between AgNCs and S and N atoms of N–S-CDs. Further decrease in the current values (I
p,a = 34 μA and I
p,c = −35 μA) and more increase in ΔE value (280 mV) are observed for Apt/AgNCs/N–S-CDs/GCE surface (curve d). This behavior corroborates the fixation of the Apt sequence on the AgNCs layer because of the increase of the spatial hindrance and repulsion between the negative charge of the oligonucleotide sequence's phosphate groups and the anion redox couple [40,41]. By incubation of 1 pM of MTZ on the BSA/Apt/AgNCs/N–S-CDs/GCE surface as the sensing interface (curve e), the I
p,a and I
p,c values are decreased to 31 μA and −32 μA, respectively, and ΔE value is increased to 310 mV. This steric/conformational restriction increase may be rooted in the fact that MTZ is trapped in the arm of the Apt attached to the surface and MTZ/Apt complex formation limits the electron transfer ability of the redox probe on the surface.

DPV signals of the modified electrode surface at each stage of the aptasensor fabrication process were recorded in the same solution of the redox probe at a range potential of −0.1 V–0.6 V. As observed in Fig. 4B, the N–S-CDs/GCE surface (curve b) exhibits a lower I
p,a value at 0.24 V compared to the unmodified GCE surface (curve a). The attachment of AgNCs on the N–S-CDs/GCE surface decreases the I
p,a value without significant displacement in the potential value (curve c). The fixation of the Apt onto the AgNCs/N–S-CDs/GCE surface is confirmed by the I
p,a decrease arising from the spatial obstruction and electrostatic repulsion increase of the sequence attached to the surface (curve d). The further inability of the electron transfer in the last step approves the incubation of MTZ as the target on the aptasensing interface and formation of the Apt/MTZ complexes with spatial hindrance (curve e).

EIS technique was applied for further investigations of the aptasensor fabrication process by recording the Nyquist curve of each electrode surface in the redox probe at a potential of 0.24 V (Fig. 4C). In this technique, the variation in the semicircle diameter as a reflecting characteristic of the resistance of the charge transfer (R
ct ) is a helpful parameter to measure the kinetic of the redox probe on the electrode interface. Nyquist curve related to unmodified GCE (curve a) in Fig. 4C illustrates a typical R
ct value of 168 Ω while the N–S-CDs/GCE (curve b) presents more R
ct value (874 Ω). This difficulty in the charge transfer may be attributed to the presence of heteroatoms by the negative partial charges in the N–S-CDs layer and their corresponding repulsion with the anion redox probe. Further increase of R
ct value to 2426 Ω for the next layer (curve c) proves blocking of the available surface area in the electron transfer reducing of the redox probe which is due to the attachment of AgNCs on the N–S-CDs/GCE surface. Bonding of Apt on the AgNCs/N–S-CDs/GCE surface via linking between the NH2 group on the 5′-terminal of the sequence and AgNCs is validated by an increase of R
ct value to 3137 Ω (curve d). This behavior can arise from the repulsion between the anion redox couple and the Apt sequence by a negative nature. By incubating 300 pM of MTZ on the prepared aptasensor surface the steric barrier of the surface is remarkably increased (R
ct = 4479 Ω, curve e) and the MTZ/Apt complex impedes the redox probe ability in the electron transferring on the aptasensor surface. Concluded to the result of the EIS, a Randles equivalent circuit is modeled by some main components including R
ct , constant phase element (CPE), solution ohmic resistance (R
s ), and Warburg impedance (W) as shown in the inset of Fig. 4C. Consequently, all of the findings obtained from the CV, DPV and EIS techniques corroborate the correct generation of the aptasensor for MTZ detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The optimum condition for the experiment
ce:section.ce:para: Achieving a stable and detectable signal is of prime importance in evaluating the aptasensor performance. To attain this purpose some experimental conditions including the used N–S-CDs amount, attachment time of N–S-CDs on the surface, and incubation time of the MTZ on the aptasensor surface were tuned. Since the Apt is a biological molecule a PB solution with a normal ionic strength of 0.1 M under a neutral condition (pH = 7.4) was selected as the optimum condition [38]. To examine the effect of N–S-CDs amount on the aptasensor response, different dilution rates of N–S-CDs with DI water (at a ratio of 1:1, 1:2: 1:4, 1:6, and 1:8) were separately injected on the GCE surface for 3 h and the corresponding DPVs were recorded. The resulting current difference (ΔI) before and after attachment of the N–S-CDs on the GCE surface in Fig. 4D reveals that the maximum signal level is obtained at the ratio of 1:2 and other ratios do not provide a sufficient amount of the N–S-CDs attached on the GCE surface. Thus, this ratio is chosen as the optimum ratio. The study of the interaction time of N–S-CDs (at the ratio of 1:2) attached on the GCE surface at a time range of 1 h–6 h in Fig. 4E exhibits the ΔI signal dramatically increases from 1 h to 3 h and then decreases. It seems that 3 h is the sufficient time for attachment of N–S-CDs on the GCE surface and a longer time leads to more accumulations on the surface and blocking of electron transfer. Therefore, 3 h is selected as the optimum attachment time of N–S-CDs.

To explore the binding period of the MTZ as the target on the Apt, the developed aptasensor was individually incubated by 1 pM of MTZ for different times of 15 min–105 min, and corresponding DPVs were recorded. The results in Fig. 4F demonstrate the value of ΔI (before and after MTZ incubation on the aptasensor surface) is dramatically increased from 15 min to 60 min and then slowly decreases. This response may be attributed to the fact that the Apt conformation change for trapping the MTZ on the sequence’ arm is completed during 60 min. More time than 60 min hinders the electron transfer of the redox probe on the surface because of more accumulations of the MTZ with steric obstruction [42,43]. So, the incubation time of 60 min is considered the optimum time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance evaluation of the MTZ aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: To appraise the analytical performance of the proposed aptasensor in the quantitative determination of MTZ, a series of different concentrations of MTZ was incubated on the aptasensor surface under optimal conditions and the corresponding DPVs were recorded in the redox probe. Fig. 4G indicates ΔI (before and after incubation of MTZ on the aptasensor surface) value is remarkably decreased by a concentration increase of MTZ. This behavior confirms the formation of more MTZ/Apt complexes by further surface space barriers on the aptasensing interface. The achieved calibration curves in Fig. 4H exhibit three good linear dynamic ranges (LDRs) for different concentrations of MTZ from 1 fM to 901.4 nM; ΔI (μA) = 2516 CMTZ + 0.3372 with a correlation coefficient (R 2) = 0.9945 for 1 fM to 1.2 pM, ΔI (μA) = 2.1399 CMTZ + 2.8212 with R 2 = 0.982 for 1.2 pM to 1.4 nM and ΔI (μA) = 0.0022 CMTZ + 4.9246 with R 2 = 0.9802 for 1.4 nM–901.4 nM. According to the low concentration range, the LOD value is obtained to be 333.3 aM based on the signal-to-noise (S/N) ratio of 3. These findings validate the analytical performance of the developed aptasensor in terms of the lowest LOD and widest LDR values compared to only previous electrochemical aptasensor [42] and also other reported MTZ sensors (Table S-1). The reasons behind this matter may be attributed to five effective characteristics of the embedded aptasensing interface: (1) GCE as the durable initial platform than other electrodes guarantees a stable surface for further attachment of other layers, (2) low-cost and green N–S-CDs strongly connects AgNCs layer on the GCE surface and also preciously reduces the fabrication cost of the aptasensor, (3) AgNCs not only provides a high specific surface area on the surface but also ensures the high-dense loading of Apt sequence on it and (4) the Apt sequence with high intrinsic affinity benefits in the target trapping enhances the selective tracing of the MTZ in the solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the stability, selectivity, repeatability, and reproducibility of the aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: The results extracted from the CVs recorded for the aptasensor incubated by 300 pM of MTZ in Fig. 4I demonstrate the preserving 96.8 % of the initial response after 80 repetitive cycles. This finding approves the reliable stability of the aptasensor. Additionally, the remaining 96.1 % of the initial response of the aptasensor after storing at 4 °C for two weeks certified the satisfactory storage stability of the aptasensor (data not shown).

A crucial factor in assessing each assay method is achieving a selective response. To evaluate the non-interference properties of the developed aptasensor some probably interfering species were analyzed by the aptasensor. The recorded DPVs for the aptasensor incubated by AMX, CFX, FA, Pen G, and UA with the same concentration of MTZ (1 pM) under the optimum condition are presented in Fig. 2S–A to F. As depicted in the bar plot in Fig. 2S–G, all tested species make a negligible change in the ΔI response compared to MTZ which is attributed to the weak and non-specific interaction of Apt with them to ban their disturbing on the aptasensing interface. This satisfactory selectivity of the aptasensor, which stems from its high-affinity property regarding MTZ and also, using BSA agent for surface blocking, may be promising in the selective assay of MTZ in complex matrices.

To ascertain the reproducibility of the aptasensor five BSA/Apt/AgNCs/N–S-CDs/GCEs as the aptasensors were separately prepared in the same way and the corresponding DPVs were recorded. The resulting bar plots for five fabricated aptasensors incubated by 300 pM MTZ in Fig. 2S–H by a relative standard deviation (RSD) value of 4.38 % reveal the reproducibility of the aptasensor is reliable. Besides, resulting bar plots for four replicative measurements for the aptasensor incubated by 300 pM MTZ with an RSD value of 2.7 % in Fig. 2S–I conclude the adequate repeatability of the applied methodology.

To appraise the feasibility of MTZ detection in real samples by the developed aptasensor, the human saliva, urine, and tap water samples prepared in the experimental part were analyzed in triplicate. DPV signals of the aptasensor incubated with the various real samples were recorded in the redox probe as the electrolyte. The calculated recovery and RSD values for triplicate detection of different concentrations of MTZ in the tested samples are listed in Table S-2. The results with a slight difference between the added and found values for each sample with three different concentrations based on the acceptable RSD values validate the aptasensor applicability in the analysis of the real complex matrixes with high repeatability and selectivity. These findings which illustrate a satisfactory assay of MTZ with sufficient and high accuracy are assigned to utilizing the advanced nanocomposite and high-affinity property of Apt regard MTZ. Additionally, the satisfactory results of the MTZ assay in the human saliva and urine samples by the proposed aptasensor compared to previous electrochemical aptasensor for MTZ assay in tap water and honey [42] manifest the applied aptasensing methodology can be considered a noninvasive approach for human biofluids.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Greenness profile of the proposed strategy
ce:section.ce:para: Since green and sustainable chemistry paves the way to change the ideal models in chemical production the logic of green chemistry points to understanding the standards of economic advancement [44,45]. The investigation of greenness index a strategy is challenging due to the numerous diverse parameters that must be considered [46,47]. The complimentary green analytical procedure index (ComplexGAPI) [45] and the analytical greenness (AGREE) index [48] are two common metrics for evaluating the greenness of the proposed approach. The software of the ComplexGAPI metrics, an innovative tool based on the GAPI, envelops the whole experimental process, including pre-analysis procedures from sample preparation and analysis to the final tests, and involves diverse aspects like yield and environmental parameters, pureness amount, instrumentation, solvents, reagents, and green economy. Similar to GAPI, the color scale of the pictogram changes from green to yellow and red based on the environmental effects with low, medium, and high grading. The results of the international ComplexGAPI index in Scheme. 1D exhibit the greenness of the proposed methodology. Due to using eggs as an environmentally friendly source, a minimal amount of the generated waste, and the low quantity of the used reagents and solvents, the applied procedure is distinguished as an eco–friendly methodology. The international AGREE metrics is another software for greenness evaluation that depends on some criteria for monitoring the environmental and occupational risks in an analytical procedure. These criteria are classified in a circular pattern like an old clock with assessment scores ranging from 0 to 1 [49] and the obtained average score at the circle center offers a benchmark of the environmental sustainability of the used procedure. The high estimated score by the AGREE software in the pictogram of Scheme. 1E reflects the greenness profile of the applied methodology.

By emphasizing using waste material to produce value-added products, the chicken ESM was used as a cost-effective available source for the easy synthesis of N–S-CDs. The physical and chemical durability of N–S-CDs together with its capability to adhere to AgNCs layer through its co-doped heteroatoms allowed high-density attachment of Apt as the specific bioreceptor regarded MTZ on the GCE surface. The resulting aptasensor delivered straightforward, sensitive monitoring of MTZ in the samples containing MTZ, promising a reliable measurement of MTZ in the real samples. It is promising from the economic aspect to generate a low-cost aptasensor because of the use of the waste ESM in the proposed aptasensing interface, unlike many expensive nanomaterials reported in many aptasensors. Besides, this idea not only generalizes to design other aptasensors for detecting various targets by Apt sequence change but also, paves the way to utilizing waste-based nanomaterials for different applications according to the green chemistry principles.

Maryam Ghiasi: Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Mahsa Ghanbarzadeh: Supervision, Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Validation, Writing – original draft. Ali Ghaffarinejad: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Faezeh Shahdost-Fard: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Supervision, Software, Investigation, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank and acknowledge the great support from Iran University of Science and Technology (IUST) and Farhangian University.

The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2024.127363.
